title: What Small Moments Build My Calm Today?
subtitle: Journal prompts for identifying the specific micro-practices that make calm reachable
excerpt: Five journal prompts for mapping the small, specific things that move the nervous system toward calm, and the equally small things that pull it back into stress. The aim is to make the pattern visible so you can choose deliberately rather than default.
sourceNotes: Adapted from the Day 25 reflection theme of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive to Fall Asleep Faster and Wake Energised (Rebecca J Page, 2025, ISBN 9798269649795).

content.text: Five journal prompts for making the daytime-calm pattern visible. The nervous system's path toward sleep starts long before bedtime. These prompts identify the specific micro-moments (the transitions, the pauses, the decisions) that either build or undermine the body's capacity for rest. Set twenty minutes and write without stopping.
New to journalling as practice? Read
Where am I right now in terms of calm, on a scale from 1 (still running at full speed) to 10 (fully settled)?
Name the number. Then write one sentence about what is keeping it from being higher.
What specific moment in today moved me toward calm?
One moment, as specific as you can make it. The task you finished, the conversation that ended well, the minute outside.
What specific moment in today pulled me back into stress?
Name it. Was it avoidable or was it fixed? Write what you would do differently if the same moment appeared tomorrow.
What one small act could I do in the next thirty minutes that would move my body toward rest?
Specific and do-able. Not 'relax' but 'make tea and sit without my phone for ten minutes'.
If I could build one daily habit that would reliably bring me back to calm, what would it be?
Write the specific habit, the specific time, and the specific cue. Not 'meditate' but 'two minutes of slow breathing immediately after the school run before opening any screens'.
Adapted from the Day 25 reflection theme of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive (Rebecca J Page, 2025).
